items.text: Lloyd's Coffee House, London — Birthplace of modern insurance business. Traders pooled money to cover marine losses.
Triton Insurance Company Limited established in Kolkata — First Non-Life (General) Insurance Company of India.
Insurance Act, 1938 — First legislation enacted to regulate and control insurance business in India. Still in force today (with amendments).
LIC (Life Insurance Corporation of India) established in September 1956 — 245 private insurance companies were merged to form LIC.
IRDA (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority) established in April 2000 — Supreme regulator of the insurance industry in India.
IRDA renamed to IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) — 'of India' added to the name.

cards.title: Insurer
cards.content: The Insurance Company — the entity that provides insurance coverage and accepts the risk.

cards.title: Insured
cards.content: The Customer / Policyholder — the person whose life or property is insured.

cards.title: Risk / Peril
cards.content: Risk means danger or threat. Peril means hazard or jeokhim (jोखिम). These terms are used interchangeably in insurance.

cards.title: Premium
cards.content: The amount periodically paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for insurance coverage.

cards.title: Life Insurance (Jeevan Bima)
cards.content: Insurance that covers the risk to human life. Main purpose: financial protection for the family if something happens to the insured.

cards.title: Non-Life Insurance (General Insurance)
cards.content: Insurance covering everything other than life — property, vehicles, marine, health, etc.

sections.title: 💡 The Concept of Insurance — Origin & How It Works

cards.title: Historical Origin (Sea Trade Concept)
cards.content: Ancient traders travelled via sea routes. Ships could sink or be looted by pirates. Before every voyage, all traders pooled a small amount of money. If anyone faced a loss, that pool was given to the affected person's family. Modern insurance is based on this exact same concept.

cards.title: How Insurance Works Today
cards.content: Multiple insured persons (customers) pay a certain amount (premium) to the insurer (company) every month. This money is pooled together. If any one insured faces a loss, the insurer pays them or their family a certain sum of money from this pool.

cards.title: Insurance = Risk Transfer
cards.content: When you take a life insurance policy, you transfer your personal risk to the insurer. Hence insurance is also called Risk Transfer.

cards.title: Insurance = Risk Transfer through Risk Pooling
cards.content: All insured persons pool their premiums with the insurer. This pooling is called Risk Pooling. Transferring risk through this pooling mechanism = Insurance. This is another official name for insurance.

cards.title: Insurance = A Method of Risk Management
cards.content: Insurance is a tool for managing financial risk. It does NOT remove the risk — it only manages the financial consequence of a loss.

cards.title: Insurance = Sharing the Losses of FEW by MANY
cards.content: In insurance, loss happens to only a few people, but the financial burden is shared by many (all premium payers). So: Insurance = Sharing the Losses of FEW by MANY. CAUTION: 'Sharing the Losses of MANY by FEW' is WRONG — do not get confused.

cards.title: Standard Numerical Formula
cards.content: Total Annual Loss = Number of losses per year × Value per asset. Annual Contribution per person = Total Annual Loss ÷ Total number of insured persons. Example: 400 houses, each worth ₹20,000; 4 houses burn per year. Total Loss = 4 × ₹20,000 = ₹80,000. Contribution per owner = ₹80,000 ÷ 400 = ₹200.

sections.title: ⚠️ What Insurance Does NOT Do (Common Misconceptions)

cards.title: Insurance does NOT protect assets
cards.content: Insurance does not physically protect your property or assets from damage.

cards.title: Insurance does NOT prevent loss
cards.content: Insurance cannot stop a loss from happening. It only compensates after the loss occurs.

cards.title: Insurance does NOT reduce the possibility of loss
cards.content: Insurance does not reduce the chances of a loss occurring.

cards.title: What Insurance DOES do ✅
cards.content: Insurance pays compensation when there is an actual loss of assets or life. It gives peace of mind and enables effective life and business planning. It is NOT a money-making tool.

cards.title: 1. Risk Avoidance
cards.content: Avoiding the activity altogether to eliminate the risk entirely. Example: Not travelling by sea to avoid the risk of shipwreck.

cards.title: 2. Loss Prevention
cards.content: Taking measures to reduce the chances of occurrence of a risk. Example: Installing fire alarms to reduce risk of fire damage.

cards.title: 3. Risk Retention
cards.content: An individual decides to bear the risk and its financial effects themselves without transferring it to anyone. They accept and absorb potential losses personally.

cards.title: 4. Risk Transfer (The BEST method)
cards.content: Transferring the financial burden of a risk to an Insurance Company by paying a premium. This is what insurance is — the best and most practical method of risk management.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Quick Memory)
items: Modern insurance originated → Lloyd's Coffee House, London (1688).
IRDAI = Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India → Established April 2000. Renamed from IRDA in 2014.
IRDAI is the ONLY regulator of the insurance industry in India — NOT RBI, NOT GIC, NOT LIC.
First form of insurance in the world → Marine Insurance.
First Non-Life Insurance Company of India → Triton Insurance Company Limited, 1850, Kolkata.
First legislation to regulate insurance in India → Insurance Act, 1938.
Insurance = Risk Transfer through Risk Pooling.
Insurance = Sharing the Losses of FEW by MANY (not Many by Few — trap question!).
Insurance pays AFTER loss — it does NOT prevent, protect, or reduce the chance of loss.
Risk Retention = bearing the risk yourself. Risk Transfer = buying insurance.
Annual Contribution formula: Total Loss ÷ Number of Insured Persons.
